data.title: Fulton Financial prices $300M subordinated notes offering
data.description:
• Fulton Financial (FULT) priced a $300M offering of fixed-to-floating rate subordinated notes due 2036.
  • The notes carry a 5.95% fixed interest rate through May 2031, then switch to a floating rate tied to SOFR plus 217 basis points.
  • Interest will be paid semi-annually during the fixed period and quarterly during the floating period.
  • The notes are callable at par starting May 15, 2031.
  • Proceeds will be used to repay $195 million of existing 3.25% subordinated notes due 2030 and for general corporate purposes.
  • The offering is expected to close around May 5, 2026.

data.title: Trident Digital Tech receives Nasdaq determination letter
data.description:
• Trident Digital Tech (TDTH) said on Friday that it received a letter dated April 28, 2026, from Nasdaq, notifying the company it has not regained compliance with Nasdaq's minimum bid price requirement rule of $1.00 per share for continued listing.
  • Nasdaq also said TDTH is not eligible for a second 180-day period to regain compliance because the company does not meet the initial listing stockholders’ equity requirement of $5M for the Nasdaq Capital Market. 
  • In addition, Nasdaq previously informed the company on March 26, 2026, that TDTH failed to maintain the minimum market value of listed securities of $35M required for continued listing for the 34 consecutive business day period from February 5, 2026, through March 20, 2026. Nasdaq afforded TDTH until September 22, 2026, to regain compliance. 
  • Nasdaq highlighted that the April 28 determination triggers a potential suspension and delisting schedule: unless TDTH requests an appeal to a Nasdaq Hearings Panel by May 5, 2026, the company’s securities will be scheduled for delisting and suspended at the opening of business on May 7, 2026, and a Form 25‑NSE will be filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission to remove the securities from Nasdaq listing and registration.
  • The company stated it intends to timely request a hearing before the Hearings Panel to present a plan to regain compliance with Rule 5550(a)(2) and to request continued listing pending its return to compliance.

data.title: iShares J.P. Morgan Broad USD Emerging Markets Bond ETF declares monthly distribution of $0.2679
data.description:
• iShares J.P. Morgan Broad USD Emerging Markets Bond ETF (BEMB) - $0.2679.
  • Payable May 06; for shareholders of record May 01; ex-div May 01.

data.title: Exodus Movement acquires Monavate and Baanx.com
data.description:
• Exodus Movement (EXOD) acquired Monavate Holdings and Baanx.com from W3C Corp receivers after a loan default.
  • The purchase price was ~$76.27M, equal to the outstanding principal and interest on W3C’s loan as of April 30, 2026.
  • Monavate and Baanx provide card issuing, processing, and regulatory infrastructure for fintech, crypto, and enterprise clients.
  • The deal enables Exodus to own its payments stack, reducing reliance on third-party providers.
  • Exodus can now issue cards via networks like Visa, Mastercard, and Discover.
  • The acquisition expands Exodus’ global reach and supports broader asset integration, including stablecoins, across the U.S., U.K., and EU.

data.title: What is next for Intel after its Q1 earnings blowout?
data.description:
Intel Corporation (INTC) shares experienced a massive 24% surge after the company delivered a decisive Q1 2026 earnings beat. The chipmaker reported quarterly revenue of $13.6 billion, representing a 7% year-over-year increase and exceeding analyst estimates for the seventh consecutive quarter. While the adjusted results were strong, with non-GAAP EPS reaching $0.29—up 123% year-over-year—the company reported a GAAP net loss of $3.7 billion due to a $3.8 billion Mobileye-related impairment charge.

Despite the explosive share price reaction and signs of an operational turnaround, analyst sentiment remains divided on whether Intel’s current valuation reflects a sustainable recovery or an overextended rally.

What Do Seeking Alpha Analysts Say About Intel’s Future?

Despite the sharp rally, several analysts maintained bullish stances on Intel, viewing the results as confirmation that the turnaround is progressing faster than anticipated.

Analysts with a positive outlook pointed to the significant momentum in the Data Center and AI segment, which saw revenues surge 22% year-over-year to $5.1 billion with operating margins reaching 30.5%. Optimism is fueled by the successful ramp of the 18A process node and strategic partnerships, including the Xeon 6 processor engagement with Google and the potential Terafab project. Proponents argue that gross margins expanding to 41% and strong demand for AI CPUs outstripping current supply demonstrate Intel’s renewed relevance in the AI infrastructure buildout.

Those with the cautious outlook, however, highlighted that the company still faces fundamental structural challenges, including persistent losses in the Foundry segment and negative free cash flow projected until 2027. Skeptics pointed to the $3.8 billion Mobileye-related impairment as a sign of volatility in subsidiaries. Furthermore, with the stock trading at a forward P/E over 87 and shares up 223% since July 2025, bears argue the risk/reward profile is unfavorable and that the recent rally has fully priced in the bull case.

Here’s a breakdown of what some analysts had to say:

  • Rick Orford, Rating: Buy: “Intel delivered a Q1 '26 revenue beat, surging 24% post-earnings, driven by strong data center and AI-related demand … despite a GAAP loss from a $3.9B Mobileye write-down masking core business improvements.” - Intel: Back On Top, But Maybe Not For Long

  • Go Finance Intelligence, Rating: Buy: “INTC’s foundry revenue reached $5.4 billion in Q1 '26, up 20% QoQ, with 18A wafers in full-volume production ramp and improving operating margins… INTC trades at a discounted 5.72x P/S versus peers.” - Intel: The Unlikely Turnaround Story

  • Dhierin Bechai, Investing Group Leader, Rating: Hold: “Despite bullish AI CPU demand trends, INTC faces execution risks, negative free cash flow until 2027, and remains loss-making in Foundry … shares now trade at a fully valued 18.3x EV/EBITDA.” - Intel: Redrawing the CPU AI Map (Rating Downgrade)

  • Deep Value Investing, Rating: Hold: “I see DCAI as the biggest surprise, with $5.1B of revenue … Xeon 6’s traction with Nvidia, Google, and SambaNova makes Intel’s AI relevance harder for me to dismiss … [but] I reiterate my hold rating until the stock retests mid-$60s.” - Intel Crushed Q1 Earnings: I’m Heading For The Exit

  • Louis Gerard, Rating: Hold: “Gross margin expanded to 41%, with Data Center and AI segment margins surging to 30.5%… [but] valuation is stretched: Forward P/E Non-GAAP at 117.78 and scenario analysis implies >50% downside from $80 levels.” - Intel: Blowout Q1 Shows Bear Case Is Dead, But At $80 Is The Bull Case Fully Priced

  • The Asian Investor, Rating: Sell: “With INTC shares up 223% since July 2025 and exceeding my $51 price target, I recommend selling into the strength, as Intel’s valuation potential is now fully realized.” - Intel: A Great Time To Take Profits (Rating Downgrade)

  • Julia Ostian, Rating: Sell: “Profitability was boosted by sales of previously reserved inventory … but high capex and negative free cash flow persist … market sentiment has driven risk far above near-term upside.” - Intel: To Short Or Not To Short, That Is The Question

What Do the Quant Ratings Say?

Intel currently carries a Hold rating from Seeking Alpha’s quant system. While the company’s operational turnaround is evident in its segment growth, the stock faces pressure from extreme valuation multiples and elevated capital expenditure requirements.

Profitability (A-): The company is showing signs of recovery in its core operations, earning a strong grade as non-GAAP gross margins expanded to 41%. However, GAAP profitability remains under significant pressure, evidenced by the $3.7 billion net loss in Q1 driven by heavy reinvestment and impairment charges.

Valuation (D-): This category receives a weak grade due to a significantly stretched forward P/E ratio of 87x, which is drastically higher than the sector median. The stock’s price-to-sales ratio has also climbed to over 8x, well above historical norms.

Growth ((A)): Growth metrics are improving but remain inconsistent. While data center and AI revenue grew 22% year-over-year, total forward revenue growth of 7% continues to lag behind some high-growth semiconductor peers.

Momentum (A+): Momentum carries a very strong grade, as the stock has surged roughly 80% year-to-date and 223% since mid-2025. However, technical indicators like the RSI reaching 83.2 suggest the stock is currently in overbought territory.

Earnings Revisions (A-): This category has seen a positive shift, receiving an improving grade following a blowout Q1 where Intel obliterated consensus estimates and provided Q2 guidance that signaled further margin and revenue expansion.

What’s the Latest News on Intel?

The semiconductor giant said its chief accounting officer, Scott Gawel, has resigned (and is taking the same post at Nvidia). Intel added that its CFO will assume the role of principal accounting officer.

data.title: Eastman targets EPS above $6 as it implements $500M in price increases amid Middle East-driven tightness
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Eastman Chemical Company (EMN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • No prepared management remarks were included; the transcript began directly with analyst Q&A.
  • Mark Costa indicated Eastman was “very excited about the strength of revenue growth associated with the new platform around methanolysis,” while also emphasizing that “the end market demand situation hasn't really changed dramatically,” and that specialty adoption was coming from wins in “Tritan sales and cosmetic packaging.”
  • William McLain framed the company’s pricing response around cost inflation and supply tightness, saying, “What we expect sequentially is in our specialties, mid-single-digit price increases from Q1 to Q2,” and “when you think about our chemical intermediates, those are phasing in... in the high teens or approaching 20%.”

Outlook

  • Mark Costa said management was not providing full-year guidance in the current environment: “that's why we're not giving full year guidance as you've got a lot of potential upside... There's obviously end market risk with inflation.”
  • Costa reiterated the circular platform growth view: “that 4% to 5% revenue growth that we've talked about in January, we still think that's probably accurate. There may be some upside.”
  • Costa provided an EPS framing for the year, stating, “our earnings per share should be above $6 a share.”
  • Costa lowered the Fibers earnings outlook versus the prior view, saying the company “lowered earnings about $20 million in our guide here,” and later specifying the segment expectation as “this $210 million to $240 million range.”

Financial Results

  • William McLain said the company “recognized about $20 million within Q1” related to IEEPA tariff refunds, adding, “There's no further IEEPA refunds to recognize,” and “we would expect to get the cash related to that sometime in the second half of the year.”
  • McLain stated the tariff refund “was basically in line with the winter storm impact,” and Mark Costa added, “the tariffs neutralize the winter storm... So it was just a clean quarter relative to how we guided in January.”
  • On near-term segment earnings, Costa said, “we sort of guided you that Q2 would be around $50 million in EBIT” for Chemical Intermediates, while cautioning that Q3 depends on “how long the market tightness goes” and “when the Strait is going to get opened.”
  • On cash and working capital, McLain said Q1 cash consumption was “lower than the prior year,” and he quantified a potential full-year working capital headwind: “I think $150 million, $200 million roughly.”

Q&A

  • Vincent Andrews, Morgan Stanley: Asked whether higher oil and virgin plastic prices were accelerating methanolysis trials and demand; Chairman & CEO Mark Costa: Said specialty demand was driven by wins and adoption, while rPET economics improved versus virgin PET, adding “we're running our capacity to serve that” and reaffirming “that 4% to 5% revenue growth... is probably accurate.”
  • Patrick Cunningham, Citigroup: Asked about market share gains and conflict-driven share upside; CEO Costa: Said in Chemical Intermediates “we can sell everything that we can make,” described improved export margins and more volume versus last year, and said specialty upside was possible but “we haven't seen a significant amount of improvement yet.”
  • Patrick Cunningham, Citigroup: Asked about Fibers shipment reductions and second-half improvement; CEO Costa: Cited Middle East customer logistics constraints, said there was “a little bit of risk in Q2,” and tied second-half improvement to contract flexibility, some build in yarn/film, energy flow-through, and cost reductions.
  • David Begleiter, Deutsche Bank: Asked if CI EBIT could reach $100 million in Q3; CEO Costa: Pointed to Q2 “around $50 million,” said Q3 could be “more similar,” and tied the range to Strait reopening timing and moderation in tightness.
  • David Begleiter, Deutsche Bank: Asked how disruptions translate into permanent specialty volume gains; CEO Costa: Said customers were learning “painful lessons about exposure and reliable suppliers,” highlighted Eastman’s “security supply advantage,” and said the company would “try and lock the business in on contracts for a longer-term commitment.”
  • Joshua Spector, UBS: Asked about validating “pull forward” and impacts on production plans; CEO Costa: Said the planning assumption was end-market demand “similar to last year,” noted low inventories exiting last year, said order books were “really strong,” and called June “a wildcard.”
  • Frank Mitsch, Fermium Research: Asked about the $500 million of price increases, customer reactions, and inflation coverage; CFO William McLain: Said specialty pricing should be “mid-single-digit” sequentially while CI is “approaching 20%,” and CEO Costa added competitors were also raising prices and Eastman expected to “get the price up, hold our volumes.”
  • Frank Mitsch, Fermium Research: Asked whether Middle East Fibers contracts could be force majeure; CEO Costa: Said Middle East customers were “about 10% of our revenue in this segment,” and emphasized the 90% base case relied on customers meeting annual contracts without a force majeure “excuse on that 90%.”
  • Matthew DeYoe, BofA: Asked if IEEPA refunds benefited Q1 or Q2 and the expected amount; CFO McLain: Said “about $20 million within Q1,” no further recognition, and cash expected in the second half.
  • Matthew DeYoe, BofA: Asked for methanolysis upscaling and reconciling “wins” with flat demand; CEO Costa: Differentiated specialty share gains from end-market growth, discussed switching a line back to PET, and said “Pepsi and some others wanted to start buying sooner,” while rPET capacity expansion “takes a bit of time.”
  • Jeffrey Zekauskas, JPMorgan: Asked if CI earnings are achievable with propane at $0.90/gallon; CFO McLain: Said pricing actions reflected the $0.75 to $0.90 range and Q2 was “approaching $50 million.”
  • Jeffrey Zekauskas, JPMorgan: Asked what is holding back operating cash flow via working capital; CFO McLain: Said inventories were built for a planned turnaround, expected depletion later, and cited inflation pressure on inventory/receivables partially mitigated by payables.
  • Kevin McCarthy, Vertical Research Partners: Asked about Advanced Materials cadence, paraxylene inflation recovery, and auto exposure; CEO Costa: Said pricing was implemented “either April 1 or May 1” for paraxylene and VAM inflation, expected sequential improvement in Q2, and said the back half could see volumes “flat to a bit better” due to circular growth and wins.
  • Kevin McCarthy, Vertical Research Partners: Asked how much harder CI assets can run; CFO McLain: Pointed to winter storm operational tailwinds into Q2, limited acetyl upside due to inventory built for a turnaround, and said margin growth was “mostly” in olefins.
  • John Roberts, Mizuho: Asked about coatings ingredients vs films in automotive weakness; CEO Costa: Said performance was similar and demand was more refinish-driven, while highlighting interlayer advantages from innovation.
  • John Roberts, Mizuho: Asked whether winter storm impacts and tariff refunds were booked in the same segments; CFO McLain: Said they were “not... uniform” but no “material difference” to highlight.
  • Michael Sison, Wells Fargo: Asked what pricing is needed in CI to get to $50 million and the year-over-year improvement; CFO McLain: Reiterated sequential CI price increases “approaching 20%,” and CEO Costa restated specialty pricing as “mid-single-digit.”
  • Michael Sison, Wells Fargo: Asked if Advanced Materials can return to 20% margins longer term; CEO Costa: Said “Absolutely,” attributing current pressure to utilization and the added methanolysis fixed cost, and said margin recovery needs “a stable economy.”
  • Arun Viswanathan, RBC: Asked whether CI tightness could cause permanent capacity rationalization; CEO Costa: Said some European, Japanese, and South Korean assets were already “on the edge,” but noted limited evidence “60 days into a crisis.”
  • Arun Viswanathan, RBC: Asked about durability of AM/AFP spread expansion; CFO William McLain: Said Eastman would make price increases “to ensure that our margins are stable” and “continue to do that in Q3,” while CEO Costa cautioned the company tracks margins on “a dollar per kg basis.”
  • Laurence Alexander, Jefferies: Asked about working capital impact on free cash flow conversion and where shortages might hit first; CFO McLain: Provided the “$150 million, $200 million roughly” working-capital headwind estimate, and CEO Costa said “We're not seeing any disruption yet at the customer level,” with limited ability to foresee shortages.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, centered on the sustainability of CI spreads, working capital pressure, and Fibers downside scenarios, including questions on “force majeure” and whether CI could reach “$100 million” EBIT in Q3.
  • Slightly positive management tone in describing pricing execution and circular wins, paired with caution on uncertainty; Costa said June is “a wildcard,” and emphasized “we're not giving full year guidance,” while still stating “our earnings per share should be above $6 a share.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, the current call’s tone shifted toward managing supply shocks and inflation from the Middle East conflict, while the prior quarter emphasized macro uncertainty, cost reductions, and multi-year initiatives such as the “E2P project.”

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter discussion was dominated by Middle East-related tightness, including Strait reopening uncertainty, with Costa repeatedly linking CI margins and potential shortages to “how long the market tightness goes”; the previous quarter focused more on demand recovery dynamics, destocking, and structural actions like E2P.
  • Eastman’s pricing posture tightened versus last quarter’s emphasis on defending value; in Q1 2026 management discussed implementing “$500 million of price increases,” with McLain citing sequential CI pricing “approaching 20%” and specialty “mid-single-digit,” while Q4 2025 centered more on cautious volume recovery and cost reduction targets.
  • Fibers narrative shifted from stabilizing contracts and rebuilding textiles in the prior quarter to conflict-linked logistics risk and a reduced earnings range in the current quarter, with Costa pointing to Middle East customers’ export constraints.

Risks and Concerns

  • Mark Costa described end-market demand as still pressured, saying “Consumer discretionary on durables is still relatively challenged,” and flagged uncertainty around June visibility: “June is a wildcard in this market context.”
  • Costa tied CI profitability sensitivity to geopolitics and logistics, highlighting uncertainty around “when the Strait is going to get opened” and the possibility that tightness moderates if it reopens.
  • Management cited Fibers risk tied to Middle East customer operations and exports, with Costa saying, “We see a little bit of risk in Q2,” while describing mitigation through contract structures and expectations that customers “historically have always met their contracts.”
  • CFO William McLain highlighted inflation-driven working capital pressure, noting “pressure on the inventory and on the receivables accounts,” and quantified a potential “$150 million, $200 million roughly” headwind.

Final Takeaway

Eastman’s Q1 2026 call centered on managing a Middle East-driven supply and inflation shock through broad price actions and a focus on secure supply, while maintaining the circular/methanolysis ramp narrative. Management reiterated expectations for 4% to 5% circular revenue growth and said EPS “should be above $6 a share,” while declining to give full-year guidance given volatility. Chemical Intermediates was positioned for a stronger Q2 around $50 million in EBIT under tight markets, while Fibers outlook was reduced to $210 million to $240 million as logistics risks and slower yarn growth weighed on expected utilization benefits.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tanger projects $2.42-$2.50 in 2026 core FFO per share as it raises guidance and targets re-tenanting upside
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Tanger Inc. (SKT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “I’m pleased to report another strong quarter for Tanger, reflecting continued momentum across our leasing, operating and marketing platforms and successful execution of our growth strategy, all contributing to our increased full year 2026 guidance.” (President, CEO & Director Stephen Yalof)

  • “Core FFO was $0.59 per share, up 11% from the prior year,” and “occupancy ended the quarter at 97%, up 120 basis points year-over-year,” while “sales productivity increased to $482 per square foot on a trailing 12-month basis and OCR remained stable at 9.7%, providing additional room for rent growth.” (CEO Yalof)

  • “In April, we announced a 7% increase in our dividend, supported by our earnings growth and conservative payout ratios.” (CEO Yalof)

  • “In the last 12 months, we executed 651 leases totaling 3.4 million square feet, representing record production for Tanger,” and “blended rent spreads of 10.5% reflect ongoing strength with re-tenanting spreads exceeding 26%.” (CEO Yalof)

  • “We are also thrilled with the success of our partnership with Unrivaled Sports,” adding, “as their exclusive shopping center partner in our shared markets, Tanger centers are on the itineraries of thousands of young athletes and their families traveling to our markets.” (CEO Yalof)

  • “We are increasingly leveraging technology to support and enhance our platform,” and “our multilingual AI chatbot now handles more than 80% of customer inquiries.” (CEO Yalof)

  • “For the first quarter, core FFO was $0.59 a share compared to $0.53 a share in the prior year period,” and “same-center NOI, which excludes lease termination income, increased 2.6% in the quarter.” (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Investment Officer Michael Bilerman)

  • “At quarter end, net debt to adjusted EBITDA was approximately 4.8x,” “all of our debt is at fixed rates,” and the company reported “over $1 billion of immediate liquidity.” (CFO Bilerman)

  • “As many of you are aware, Mr. Tanger will be retiring from our Board next week.” (CEO Yalof)

  • “Next week, as previously announced, I will retire from Tanger’s Board and step into the role of Chair Emeritus.” (Chair Emeritus Steven Tanger)

Outlook

  • “We have increased our full year 2026 guidance and now expect core FFO per share in a range of $2.42 to $2.50,” and “same-center NOI growth guidance remains at 2.25% to 4.25% for the year.” (CFO Bilerman)

  • “Our guidance does not assume any additional acquisitions, dispositions or financing activity beyond what has already been completed to date.” (CFO Bilerman)

  • “We’re very optimistic about our ability to drive rent in our shopping centers,” and “we’re anticipating about 80% of our -- to renew about 80% of our roll this year.” (CEO Yalof)

Financial Results

  • “Same-center NOI, which excludes lease termination income, increased 2.6% in the quarter,” and “the quarter’s NOI growth was impacted by elevated snow removal costs.” (CFO Bilerman)

  • “The snow relative to last year, you got about $0.01 that impacted it year-over-year,” and “probably about 100 basis points to that same-center growth in the first quarter.” (CFO Bilerman)

  • “Our interest coverage remains strong,” and “our interest coverage remains strong. All of our debt is at fixed rates, inclusive of our swaps with a weighted average interest rate of about 4% and a weighted average term to maturity of approximately 4.5 years once our upcoming near-term maturities are addressed.” (CFO Bilerman)

  • “We currently have over $1 billion of immediate liquidity,” including flexibility to address “the $350 million of unsecured bonds that come due this September” and “the potential early redemption of our $115 million mortgage in Kansas City.” (CFO Bilerman)

Q&A

  • Andrew Reale, BofA Securities: “Do you expect re-tenanting spreads can continue to sort of run in this mid-20% area… and… remind us what the retention rate is today?” CEO Yalof: “We’re very optimistic about our ability to drive rent,” and “we’re anticipating… about 80%… to renew… this year,” adding, “our retenanting spreads are far higher than our renewal spreads.”

  • Andrew Reale, BofA Securities: “Could you quantify where that same-store growth would have been ex the snow removal… and… swing factors…?” CFO Bilerman: “You got about $0.01 that impacted it year-over-year,” citing variability including “percentage rents,” “downtime,” and “an uncertain macro environment.”

  • Craig Mailman, Citi: asked about lease termination economics and whether it was tied to F&B initiatives. CFO Bilerman: “These are negotiated transactions,” and “we were able to bank that and then go ahead and re-lease the space.” CEO Yalof: “The F&B piece of our business has been a really important strategy,” tying it to sports tourism families who “want a place to dine and be entertained.”

  • Michael Griffin, Evercore: asked about gas prices and shopping patterns. CEO Yalof: “I’m really impressed with how resilient our customers have been,” and “we still were able to drive an increase in sales and an increase in traffic in the first quarter.”

  • Michael Griffin, Evercore: asked about acquisition opportunity set and returns. CFO Bilerman: “Our pipeline remains active,” and “it’s not that initial yield. It’s the growth that can be attained over time.”

  • Juan Sanabria, BMO: asked about bankruptcies/closures and the cadence through the year. CFO Bilerman: “We would expect 2Q to have most of the brunt of that,” with temp/permanent backfills “into the back half of the year.”

  • Todd Thomas, KeyBanc: asked about hybrid mix and OCR implications. CEO Yalof: “We’re going to determine which centers have the opportunity to be more hybrid,” and “at 9.7%, I think there’s still a lot of headroom for us to continue to grow rents.”

  • Caitlin Burrows, Goldman Sachs: asked what could drive future equity issuance and leasing demand breadth. CFO Bilerman: “It’s all about being prudent and disciplined,” and cited “between $80 million and $100 million of free cash flow after our dividends.” CEO Yalof: “Footwear and apparel… now down to 70%,” as Tanger pursues “entertainment,” “health and beauty,” “food,” and “home goods.”

  • Naishal Shah, Green Street: asked about 2026 OpEx and temp tenancy levels. CFO Bilerman: “We guide same-center NOI,” and on temp, “we’re about 10% today,” and “we’ll probably have a little bit higher temp as we move through some of the bankruptcies.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, pressing on spread durability, snow-cost impacts, bankruptcy cadence, acquisition competitiveness, and capital needs, including questions like “what sort of cap rates or IRRs are you targeting?” (Greg McGinniss, Scotiabank) and “how should we incorporate that in our forecast?” (Juan Sanabria, BMO).

  • Management’s tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks, emphasizing “another strong quarter,” “record production,” and raised guidance, while remaining measured in Q&A around macro uncertainty and timing risks, including “we still have an uncertain macro environment.” (CFO Bilerman)

  • Versus the prior quarter, the current call maintained a similar confidence level on leasing demand and the “limited new retail development” backdrop, but added more specific near-term phasing language on bankruptcies, including “2Q… most of the brunt.” (CFO Bilerman)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was raised from “core FFO per share in the range of $2.41 to $2.49” (Q4 2025) to “$2.42 to $2.50” (Q1 2026), while same-center NOI growth guidance stayed “2.25% to 4.25%.” (CFO Bilerman)

  • Occupancy shifted from “98.1%” at year-end (Q4 2025) to “97%” at Q1 quarter-end, alongside management’s reminder that “sequential change was due primarily to seasonal patterns.” (CEO Yalof)

  • The temp leasing strategy remained a recurring theme, with “10-ish percent” discussed previously and reiterated as “about 10% today,” with an expectation of “a little bit higher temp” near-term tied to bankruptcies. (CFO Bilerman)

  • Management change messaging was new in the current quarter, with “Mr. Tanger will be retiring from our Board next week,” transitioning to “Chair Emeritus.” (CEO Yalof; Chair Emeritus Tanger)

Risks and Concerns

  • “We still have an uncertain macro environment,” and management cited variability tied to “sales,” “percentage rents,” and “the downtime and the ability to bring those tenants in on time.” (CFO Bilerman)

  • On tenant credit, guidance positioning assumed disruption timing, with “2Q… most of the brunt of that,” mitigated by executed “backfill deals either on a permanent basis or a short-term temp basis.” (CFO Bilerman)

  • On expenses, “elevated snow removal costs” were identified as a quarterly headwind, with the company emphasizing ongoing cost discipline. (CFO Bilerman)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a quarter of higher year-over-year core FFO and raised full-year core FFO guidance, supported by high occupancy, stable OCR, record leasing volume over the last 12 months, and a strategy of renewing fewer tenants to pursue higher re-tenanting spreads. Executives highlighted balance sheet capacity with fixed-rate debt and over $1 billion of liquidity to address near-term maturities, while framing bankruptcies and closures as manageable within the existing same-center NOI guidance range and expecting the heaviest impact in 2Q. The call also introduced a board transition with Steven Tanger’s retirement and move to Chair Emeritus.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Stocks to watch on Friday after hours: CTLP, CELU, ULH
data.description:
The stock market finished Friday's session modestly higher as Wall Street kicked off the month of May on an optimistic note, with oil prices retreating and corporate earnings going strong.

The S&P 500 (SP500) rose 0.3% after closing above the 7.2K level for the first time ever in the prior session, and the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) -- crossing the 25K mark for the first time—gained 0.9%. Meanwhile, the Dow (DJI) edged down 0.3%.

Now here are the 3 stocks to watch on Friday after hours—usually a quiet session ahead of the weekend:

  • Cantaloupe (CTLP) stock rose 1.6% in after-hours trading after the Federal Trade Commission approved 365 Retail's planned $848M purchase of the company with a required divestiture.
  • Celularity (CELU) shares gained 4.5% after disclosing it regained Nasdaq compliance.
  • Universal Logistics (ULH) shares dipped 1.4% in extended trading after posting Q1 GAAP EPS of -$0.13 (missing consensus by $0.22) on operating revenue of $367.6M (missing by $4.7M).

data.title: FLUENT to sell Texas cannabis operations for $30M
data.description:
• FLUENT Corp. (CSE: FNT.U; OTCQB: CNTMF) on Friday said it has entered into a definitive agreement to sell its Texas business, including cultivation, manufacturing, delivery and retail operations, to Legacy Therapeutics for $30 million.
  • Under the deal, $25 million will be paid at closing, with two additional $2.5 million payments due on the first and second anniversaries of completion.
  • The company said proceeds are expected to be used primarily to repay a portion of its senior secured debt, with remaining funds allocated to working capital and general corporate purposes.
  • The transaction, which is subject to regulatory approvals and other closing conditions, involves the transfer of FLUENT’s subsidiary Cansortium Texas, which operates in Schulenburg and Houston.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Amg expects Q2 2026 adjusted EBITDA of $290M-$305M and plans about $500M of 2026 buybacks, backed by record Q1 alternative flows
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Affiliated Managers Group (AMG) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "AMG reported record results for the first quarter with adjusted EBITDA of approximately $317 million and economic earnings per share of $8.23, representing year-over-year growth of 39% and 58%, respectively," said (President, CEO & Director Jay Horgen).
  • "Rising demand for liquid alternative strategies and ongoing strength in private markets fundraising generated record quarterly net client cash flows of more than $22 billion," said (President, CEO & Director Horgen), adding it "bring[s] net flows over the last 12 months to $52 billion, an organic growth rate of 7% over the period."
  • "In the quarter... we repurchased shares at an elevated pace, deploying approximately $186 million," said (President, CEO & Director Horgen), and said this brought "share buybacks over the past 12 months to more than $700 million, a reduction of 10% in our shares outstanding."
  • "In January, we completed our investment in BBH Credit Partners," and "in February, we announced a new partnership with Highbrook Investors" plus "an incremental minority investment in Garda Capital Partners," said (President, CEO & Director Horgen).
  • "AMG's AUM was $882 billion, the highest level in our history," said (Chief Financial Officer Dava Ritchea), and "net client cash inflows of more than $22 billion marked our fourth consecutive quarter of positive and increasing net flows."

Outlook

  • "We expect adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $290 million to $305 million... including seasonably lower net performance fees of up to $10 million," said (Chief Financial Officer Ritchea).
  • "Assuming an adjusted weighted average share count of 26.7 million, we expect second quarter economic earnings per share to be between $7.60 and $8.01," said (Chief Financial Officer Ritchea), adding that "the midpoint... represents approximately 45% growth versus Q2 2025."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s tone on Q1, the company previously guided, "We expect adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $310 million to $330 million... including net performance fees of $40 million to $60 million," said (Chief Financial Officer Ritchea) on the Q4 2025 call.

Financial Results

  • "We reported adjusted EBITDA of $317 million, which grew 39% year-over-year," said (Chief Financial Officer Ritchea).
  • "Fee-related earnings... grew 29% year-over-year," said (Chief Financial Officer Ritchea), attributing it to "positive organic growth, the positive impact of investment performance and margin expansion at some of our largest affiliates."
  • "Net performance fee earnings of $49 million in the quarter increased $29 million from the prior year," said (Chief Financial Officer Ritchea), citing "Capula, Winton, AQR and ValueAct."
  • On mix and flows by channel, "in liquid alternatives, our affiliates generated $25 billion in net inflows," and "our private market affiliates raised $4 billion in the quarter," while "in equities, net outflows of approximately $9 billion" weighed on results, said (Chief Financial Officer Ritchea).

Q&A

  • William Katz, TD Cowen: Asked to "dig in" on the "4 verticals of opportunity" and "April" momentum; (President, CEO & Director Horgen) said, "our flows were broad-based," adding, "in the quarter, we had $29 billion in Alternative flows... Over the past year, we generated $90 billion in flows into alternatives," and said "in April, we've seen strong beta... our assets are at another all-time high."
  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: Asked about wealth-channel appetite for Pantheon products amid "turbulence" and the "road map" for new products; (Chief Financial Officer Ritchea) said, "we remain constructive on the secular trend," and highlighted Pantheon vehicles including "P-BUILD," "P-SECC," and "P-PEXX," while (President, CEO & Director Horgen) said, "this is actually an opportunity to differentiate" and cited "the newly registered BBH Opportunity Credit Fund."
  • Daniel Fannon, Jefferies: Asked if market dislocations create more opportunity for new investments; (President, CEO & Director Horgen) said, "public market valuations for alternatives are way down," and added, "we have an expectation" this will "trickle into the M&A market" and "pricing has gotten better for us as we look forward."
  • Brian Bedell, Deutsche Bank: Pressed on tax-aware strategies’ contribution and distribution-platform dynamics; (President, CEO & Director Horgen) said tax-loss harvesting "has been around... since 1993," and added, "Long/short strategy in wealth account for just 8% of our assets under management" and "less than 8% of our EBITDA last year in the first quarter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative (analysts): Analysts repeatedly returned to concentration and distribution questions, including, "the investment community... has gotten myopically focused on AQR," (William Katz, TD Cowen) and "we... are getting more questions on the tax aware strategy" (Brian Bedell, Deutsche Bank).
  • Slightly positive (management): Management emphasized breadth and continuity, including "our flows were broad-based" and "we are not aware of anything that changes our positive outlook" (President, CEO & Director Horgen), and "we are confident in our ability to generate meaningful incremental value over time" (Chief Financial Officer Ritchea).
  • Versus last quarter: In Q4 2025, management’s tone was more celebratory and transition-focused, including "one of the strongest years in our company's history" (CEO & Director Jay Horgen) and a senior leadership change: "Tom has informed us... he is ready to take a next step in his career" (CEO & Director Horgen).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Growth narrative shifted from full-year recap to record quarterly execution: Q4 2025 emphasized "full year economic earnings per share of $26.05" and "approximately $29 billion in annual net client cash flows" (CEO & Director Jay Horgen), while Q1 2026 emphasized "record quarterly net client cash flows of more than $22 billion" and "record assets under management and record fee-related EBITDA" (President, CEO & Director Horgen).
  • The flow discussion broadened in Q1 2026 away from single-firm framing: "AMG is truly a diverse business" (President, CEO & Director Horgen) versus Q4 2025’s heavier emphasis that "Pantheon and AQR are our 2 largest and long-standing affiliates" (CEO & Director Horgen).
  • Capital return remained central, but Q1 2026 increased specificity for the year: "for the full year, we expect to repurchase approximately $500 million" (Chief Financial Officer Ritchea) versus Q4 2025, "we anticipate repurchasing at least $400 million in shares in 2026" (Chief Financial Officer Ritchea).

Risks and Concerns

  • On product/channel noise and potential spillover, management said it was "monitoring the broader industry dynamics" (Chief Financial Officer Ritchea) and noted "near-term growth may be more muted due to traditional direct lending trends" for P-SECC (Chief Financial Officer Ritchea).
  • On equity-strategy pressure, "net outflows of approximately $9 billion... reflected ongoing industry and performance headwinds," said (Chief Financial Officer Ritchea), while highlighting "pockets of strength" including "consistent positive net flows at Artemis" (Chief Financial Officer Ritchea).

Final Takeaway

AMG management described Q1 2026 as a record quarter led by alternatives-driven inflows, record AUM, and year-over-year earnings growth, while reiterating that flows were “broad-based” across multiple alternative verticals rather than dependent on any single affiliate or product. Management paired that message with Q2 guidance for lower seasonal performance fees and reiterated an ongoing capital return plan, alongside continued deployment into new and existing affiliate partnerships, positioning capital allocation and alternatives mix-shift as the primary levers shaping earnings power going forward.
